mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 518 or 518i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 518/518i engine typically costs £500–£3,500 supply only depending on which generation, with used units available from £400–£1,200. Fitting adds £700–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

It depends on which generation. The M10 and M40 engines are robust but require regular timing belt changes — a snapped belt is catastrophic. The M43 engines (1994–2001) are significantly more reliable thanks to the timing chain. The M43B19 in the E39 is the most refined and dependable of the four-cylinder 5 Series.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 518 and 518i?

acceptedAnswer.text: M10: timing belt failure (requires regular replacement). M40: timing belt failure and ticking hydraulic lifters. M43: cooling system leaks and head gasket issues. The M43 is the most reliable of the family.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 518 / 518i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well-kept E39 518i worth £2,000–£5,000, a £1,500–£2,800 recon is 30–60% — borderline but often worth it on a cherished example. Against a tired E12 518 worth £1,000, a £1,000 recon exceeds 70% — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 518 or 518i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. E12 518 (1974–1981) uses M10B18 carb; E28 518i (1981–1987) uses M10B18 injection; E34 518i (1989–1994) uses M40B18; E34 518i (1994–1995) uses M43B18; E39 518i (1996–2001) uses M43B19. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M10 / M40 timing belt, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timing belt must be replaced — it's a wear item. Replacement interval is 40,000–60,000 miles or 4–5 years. If the belt snaps, the engine is typically destroyed. A preventative belt replacement (£300–£600) is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£500–£1,800+). On M43 engines, the timing chain replaces the belt and is much more reliable.

problems.title: Timing Belt Failure (M10, M40)
problems.symptoms: Engine stops suddenly, ticking from the front of the engine, complete loss of power, catastrophic engine failure

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the belt fails, the engine stops instantly and internal damage is highly likely

problems.recommendation: The M10 and M40 engines use timing belts — not chains. BMW recommended replacement every 40,000–60,000 miles or 4–5 years. A snapped timing belt on these interference engines means valve and piston damage — effectively an engine replacement. If you're buying an older 518/518i with an M10 or M40 engine, demand proof of timing belt replacement. Budget for it immediately if there's no evidence. The M40 is particularly known for ticking lifters and belt wear.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing belt failure guide

problems.title: Cooling System and Head Gasket Issues (All Engines)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, rising temperature gauge, white smoke from exhaust, "mayo" under the oil filler cap, overheating
problems.recommendation: Like many older BMW engines, the M10, M40 and M43 can suffer from cooling system neglect. A failed water pump, clogged radiator, or old coolant can lead to overheating and head gasket failure. On the M43, cooling system leaks are a known issue — one owner reported losing half a litre of coolant every 5–8 days. Regular coolant changes and water pump replacement are essential. Catch overheating early and it's a manageable repair; ignore it and the head gasket fails.

failureLink.label: Read the full head gasket failure guide

problems.title: M40 Ticking Lifters and Camshaft Wear
problems.symptoms: Loud ticking noise from the engine, especially when cold or at idle, loss of power, rough running
problems.recommendation: The M40 engine is notorious for ticking hydraulic lifters — they can sound like a sewing machine when revved. In some cases, the camshaft can wear excessively, limiting revs to 4,000–5,000 rpm. Regular oil changes with the correct grade can help. If the ticking becomes severe or the engine won't rev freely, have the lifters and camshaft inspected. Replacement is a well-understood job for any BMW specialist.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 518 / 518i? Check These First
buyIf: * M43-engined car (1994–2001) — the timing chain makes these significantly more reliable than belt-driven M10/M40 cars
* M10 or M40 with documented timing belt replacement within the last 40,000–60,000 miles — the single most important check
* Full service history with regular oil changes — these engines are robust but ageing
* Evidence of cooling system maintenance — water pump and coolant changes
avoidIf: * M10 or M40 with no proof of timing belt replacement — budget for it immediately or walk away
* M40 with severe ticking that doesn't go away — could indicate camshaft wear
* Any signs of overheating or coolant loss — head gasket or cooling system issues are likely
* Poor service history — these engines are forgiving but not indestructible

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 518/518i badge spans 27 years and four engine families. The early M10 and M40 engines are simple, robust, but demand timing belt attention — a snapped belt is catastrophic. The M43 engines (1994–2001) are significantly more reliable thanks to the timing chain. If you're buying an M10 or M40 car, demand timing belt history. If you're buying an M43, you're getting BMW's most refined four-cylinder of the era. Buy on history, not on price.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 518 or 518i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Timing Belt History (M10/M40) — Has it been changed within the last 40,000–60,000 miles or 4–5 years?
2. Cold Start — Any ticking that doesn't go away? (M40 lifter or camshaft warning)
3. Service History — including which engine era and evidence of timing belt/chain work
4. Coolant Level — Any loss or signs of "mayo"? (head gasket or cooling system warning)
5. Oil Condition — Any signs of coolant contamination? (milky appearance)
6. Temperature Gauge — Any history of overheating?
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)
